([/ˈrɒməni, ˈroʊ-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*ROM-ə-nee, ROH-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) also **Romanes** [/ˈrɒmənɪs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*ROM-ən-iss*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[16]](#cite_note-16) **Romany**, **Roma**; Romani: *rromani ćhib*) is an [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") macrolanguage of the [Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people").[[17]](#cite_note-17) The largest Romani dialects are [Vlax Romani](/wiki/Vlax_Romani_language "Vlax Romani language") (about 500,000 speakers),[[18]](#cite_note-rmy_ethno-18) [Balkan Romani](/wiki/Balkan_Romani "Balkan Romani") (600,000),[[19]](#cite_note-rmn_ethno-19) and [Sinte Romani](/wiki/Sinte_Romani "Sinte Romani") (300,000).[[20]](#cite_note-rmo_ethno-20) Some Romani communities speak [mixed languages](/wiki

**Romani** ([/ˈrɒməni, ˈroʊ-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*ROM-ə-nee, ROH-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) also **Romanes** [/ˈrɒmənɪs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*ROM-ən-iss*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"),[[16]](#cite_note-16) **Romany**, **Roma**; Romani: *rromani ćhib*) is an [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") macrolanguage of the [Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people").[[17]](#cite_note-17) The largest Romani dialects are [Vlax Romani](/wiki/Vlax_Romani_language "Vlax Romani language") (about 500,000 speakers),[[18]](#cite_note-rmy_ethno-18) [Balkan Romani](/wiki/Balkan_Romani "Balkan Romani") (600,000),[[19]](#cite_note-rmn_ethno-19) and [Sinte Romani](/wiki/Sinte_Romani "Sinte Romani") (300,000).[[20]](#cite_note-rmo_ethno-20) Some Romani communities speak [mixed languages](/wiki/Mixed_language "Mixed language") based on the surrounding language with retained Romani-derived vocabulary – these are known by linguists as [Para-Romani](/wiki/Para-Romani "Para-Romani") varieties, rather than dialects of the Romani language itself.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The differences between the various varieties can be as large as, for example, the differences between the [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages").[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová199323-22)

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

Speakers of the Romani language usually refer to the language as **rromani ćhib** "the Romani language" or **rromanes* (adverb)* "in a Rom way". This derives from the Romani word **rrom**, meaning either "a member of the (Romani) group" or "husband". This is also the origin of the term "Roma" in English, although some Roma groups refer to themselves using other [demonyms](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") (e.g. 'Kaale', 'Sinti').[[23]](#cite_note-hancock-23)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Classification")]

In the 18th century, it was shown by comparative studies that Romani belongs to the Indo-European language family.[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24)
In 1763 István Vályi, a [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") pastor from [Satu Mare](/wiki/Satu_Mare "Satu Mare") in [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), was the first to notice the similarity between Romani and [Indo-Aryan languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") by comparing the Romani dialect of [Győr](/wiki/Gy%C5%91r "Győr") with the language (perhaps [Sinhala](/wiki/Sinhala_language "Sinhala language")) spoken by three [Sri Lankan](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") students he met in the Netherlands.[[25]](#cite_note-25) This was followed by the linguist Johann Christian Christoph Rüdiger (1751–1822) whose book **Von der Sprache und Herkunft der Zigeuner aus Indien** (1782) posited that Romani was descended from [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit"). This prompted the philosopher [Christian Jakob Kraus](/wiki/Christian_Jakob_Kraus "Christian Jakob Kraus") to collect linguistic evidence by systematically interviewing the Roma in [Königsberg](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsberg "Königsberg") prison. Kraus never published his findings, but they may have influenced or laid the groundwork for later linguists, especially [August Pott](/wiki/August_Pott "August Pott") and his pioneering **Darstellung der Zigeuner in Europa und Asien** (1844–45).

By the mid-nineteenth century the linguist and author [George Borrow](/wiki/George_Borrow "George Borrow") was able to state categorically his findings that it was a language with its origins in India, and he later published a glossary, *Romano Lavo-lil*.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Research into the way the Romani dialects branched out was started in 1872 by the [Slavicist](/wiki/Slavic_studies "Slavic studies") [Franz Miklosich](/wiki/Franz_Miklosich "Franz Miklosich") in a series of essays. However, it was the philologist [Ralph Turner](/wiki/Ralph_Lilley_Turner "Ralph Lilley Turner")'s 1927 article "The Position of Romani in Indo-Aryan" that served as the basis for the integration of Romani into the history of Indian languages.

Romani is an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") that is part of the [Balkan sprachbund](/wiki/Balkan_sprachbund "Balkan sprachbund"). Romani is the only [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages#New_Indo-Aryan "Indo-Aryan languages") language, other than [Domari](/wiki/Domari_language "Domari language"), to be spoken exclusively outside the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent").[[27]](#cite_note-intro-27) The Romani language has considerable influence from [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language") and [Byzantine Greek](/wiki/Byzantine_Greek "Byzantine Greek").[[28]](#cite_note-:3-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) [South Slavic](/wiki/South_Slavic_languages "South Slavic languages") influence is also notable, but is principally limited to grammar and [phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Romani is sometimes classified in the [Central Zone](/wiki/Central_Indo-Aryan_languages "Central Indo-Aryan languages") or [Northwestern Zone](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages#Northwestern_Zone "Indo-Aryan languages") Indo-Aryan languages, and sometimes treated as a group of its own.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)
Romani shares a number of features with the Central Zone languages.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33) The most significant [isoglosses](/wiki/Isogloss "Isogloss") are the shift of Old Indo-Aryan *r̥* to *u* or *i* ([Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") **śr̥ṇ-**, Romani **šun-** 'to hear') and *kṣ-* to *kh* (Sanskrit **akṣi**, Romani **j-akh** 'eye').[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33) However, unlike other Central Zone languages, Romani preserves many dental clusters (Romani **trin** 'three', **phral** 'brother', compare [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") **tīn**, **bhāi**).[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33) This implies that Romani split from the Central Zone languages before the [Middle Indo-Aryan period](/wiki/Middle_Indo-Aryan_languages "Middle Indo-Aryan languages").[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33) However, Romani shows some features of New Indo-Aryan, such as erosion of the original nominal case system towards a nominative/oblique dichotomy, with new grammaticalized case suffixes added on.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33) This means that the Romani exodus from India could not have happened until late in the first millennium.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33)

Many words are similar to the [Marwari](/wiki/Marwari_language "Marwari language") and [Lambadi](/wiki/Lambadi "Lambadi") languages spoken in large parts of India. Romani also shows some similarity to the Northwestern Zone languages.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33) In particular, the grammaticalization of enclitic pronouns as person markers on verbs (**kerdo** 'done' + **me** 'me' → **kerdjom** 'I did') is also found in languages such as [Kashmiri](/wiki/Kashmiri_language "Kashmiri language") and [Shina](/wiki/Shina_language "Shina language").[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33) This evidences a northwest migration during the split from the Central Zone languages consistent with a later migration to Europe.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33)

Based on these data, [Yaron Matras](/wiki/Yaron_Matras "Yaron Matras")[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006-34) views Romani as "kind of Indian hybrid: a central Indic dialect that had undergone partial convergence with northern Indic languages."[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33)

In terms of its grammatical structures, Romani is conservative in maintaining almost intact the Middle Indo-Aryan present-tense person concord markers, and in maintaining consonantal endings for nominal case – both features that have been eroded in most other modern Indo-Aryan languages.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33)

Romani shows a number of phonetic changes that distinguish it from other Indo-Aryan languages – in particular, the devoicing of voiced aspirates (*bh dh gh* > *ph th kh*), shift of medial *t d* to *l*, of short *a* to *e*, initial *kh* to *x*, rhoticization of retroflex *ḍ, ṭ, ḍḍ, ṭṭ, ḍh* etc. to *r* and *ř*, and shift of inflectional *-a* to *-o*.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33)

After leaving the Indian subcontinent, Romani was heavily affected by contact with European languages.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33) The most significant of these was [Medieval Greek](/wiki/Medieval_Greek "Medieval Greek"), which contributed lexically, phonemically, and grammatically to Early Romani (10th–13th centuries).[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33) This includes inflectional affixes for nouns, and verbs that are still productive with borrowed vocabulary, the shift to [VO word order](/wiki/VO_language "VO language"), and the adoption of a preposed definite article.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33) Early Romani also borrowed from [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language") and [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language").[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33)

Romani and [Domari](/wiki/Domari_language "Domari language") share some similarities: agglutination of postpositions of the second layer (or case marking clitics) to the nominal stem, concord markers for the past tense, the neutralisation of gender marking in the plural, and the use of the oblique case as an accusative.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) This has prompted much discussion about the relationships between these two languages. Domari was once thought to be the "sister language" of Romani, the two languages having split after the departure from the Indian subcontinent, but more recent research suggests that the differences between them are significant enough to treat them as two separate languages within the Central Zone ([Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language")) group of languages. The Dom and the Rom therefore likely descend from two different migration waves out of India, separated by several centuries.[[37]](#cite_note-What_is_Domari-37)[[38]](#cite_note-ROMANI_ORIGINS-38)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [Early Romani](/wiki/Early_Romani "Early Romani")

The first attestation of Romani is from 1542 AD in western Europe.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33) The earlier history of the Romani language is completely undocumented, and is understood primarily through comparative linguistic evidence.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006History-33)

Linguistic evaluation carried out in the nineteenth century by Pott (1845) and Miklosich (1882–1888) showed the Romani language to be a New Indo-Aryan language (NIA), not a [Middle Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Middle_Indo-Aryan_languages "Middle Indo-Aryan languages") (MIA), establishing that the ancestors of the Romani could not have left India significantly earlier than 1000 AD.

The principal argument favouring a migration during or after the transition period to NIA is the loss of the old system of nominal case, and its reduction to just a two-way case system, nominative vs. oblique. A secondary argument concerns the system of gender differentiation. Romani has only two [genders](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") (masculine and feminine). Middle Indo-Aryan languages (named MIA) generally had three genders (masculine, feminine and neuter), and some modern Indo-Aryan languages retain this old system even today.

It is argued that loss of the neuter gender did not occur until the transition to NIA. Most of the neuter nouns became masculine while a few feminine, like the neuter अग्नि (**agni**) in the [Prakrit](/wiki/Prakrit "Prakrit") became the feminine आग (**āg**) in [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") and **jag** in Romani. The parallels in grammatical gender evolution between Romani and other NIA languages have been cited as evidence that the forerunner of Romani remained on the Indian subcontinent until a later period, perhaps even as late as the tenth century.

There is no historical proof to clarify who the ancestors of the Romani were or what motivated them to emigrate from the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), but there are various theories. The influence of [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), and to a lesser extent of [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language") and the [Iranian languages](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages") (like [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurdish_languages "Kurdish languages")) points to a prolonged stay in [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia"), Armenian highlands/Caucasus after the departure from South Asia. The latest territory where Romani is thought to have been spoken as a mostly unitary linguistic variety is the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), between the 10th and the 13th centuries. The language of this period, which can be reconstructed on the basis of modern-day dialects, is referred to as *[Early Romani](/wiki/Early_Romani "Early Romani")* or *Late Proto-Romani*.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras200219-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

The [Mongol invasion of Europe](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Europe "Mongol invasion of Europe") beginning in the first half of the thirteenth century triggered another westward migration. The Romani arrived in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and afterwards spread to the other continents. The great distances between the scattered Romani groups led to the development of local community distinctions. The differing local influences have greatly affected the modern language, splitting it into a number of different (originally exclusively regional) dialects.

Today, Romani is spoken by small groups in 42 European countries.[[42]](#cite_note-ema-42) A project at [Manchester University](/wiki/Manchester_University "Manchester University") in England is transcribing Romani dialects, many of which are on the brink of extinction, for the first time.[[42]](#cite_note-ema-42)

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Dialects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Romany_dialects_Europe.svg/330px-Romany_dialects_Europe.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Romany_dialects_Europe.svg)

Dialects of the Romani language

Today's dialects of Romani are differentiated by the vocabulary accumulated since their departure from [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia"), as well as through [divergent phonemic evolution](/wiki/Sound_change "Sound change") and grammatical features. Many Roma no longer speak the language or speak various new [contact languages](/wiki/Contact_language "Contact language") from the local language with the addition of Romani vocabulary.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Dialect differentiation began with the dispersal of the Romani from the Balkans around the 14th century and on, and with their settlement in areas across Europe in the 16th and 17th centuries.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Dialect_diversity-44) The two most significant areas of divergence are the southeast (with epicenter of the northern Balkans) and west-central Europe (with epicenter Germany).[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Dialect_diversity-44) The central dialects replace **s** in grammatical paradigms with **h**.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Dialect_diversity-44) The northwestern dialects append **j-**, simplify **ndř** to **r**, retain **n** in the nominalizer **-ipen** / **-iben**, and lose adjectival past-tense in intransitives (**gelo**, **geli** → **geljas** 'he/she went').[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Dialect_diversity-44) Other isoglosses (esp. demonstratives, 2/3pl perfective concord markers, loan verb markers) motivate the division into Balkan, Vlax, Central, Northeast, and Northwest dialects.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Dialect_diversity-44)

Matras (2002, 2005) has argued for a theory of geographical classification of Romani dialects, which is based on the diffusion in space of innovations. According to this theory, [Early Romani](/wiki/Early_Romani "Early Romani") (as spoken in the Byzantine Empire) was brought to western and other parts of Europe through population migrations of Rom in the 14th–15th centuries. These groups settled in the various European regions during the 16th and 17th centuries, acquiring fluency in a variety of contact languages. Changes emerged then, which spread in wave-like patterns, creating the dialect differences attested today. According to Matras, there were two major centres of innovations: some changes emerged in western Europe (Germany and vicinity), spreading eastwards; other emerged in the Wallachian area, spreading to the west and south. In addition, many regional and local isoglosses formed, creating a complex wave of language boundaries. Matras points to the prothesis of **j-** in **aro** > **jaro** 'egg' and **ov** > **jov** 'he' as typical examples of west-to-east diffusion, and of addition of prothetic **a-** in **bijav** > **abijav** as a typical east-to-west spread. His conclusion is that dialect differences formed in situ, and not as a result of different waves of migration.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

According to this classification, the dialects are split as follows:

* [Northern Romani dialects](/wiki/Northern_Romani_dialects "Northern Romani dialects") in western and northern Europe, southern Italy and the Iberian peninsula[[46]](#cite_note-matras_proceedings-46)
* [Central Romani dialects](/wiki/Central_Romani_dialects "Central Romani dialects") from southern [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), [Carpathian Ruthenia](/wiki/Carpathian_Ruthenia "Carpathian Ruthenia") and southeastern [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria")[[46]](#cite_note-matras_proceedings-46)
* [Balkan Romani dialects](/wiki/Balkan_Romani "Balkan Romani"), including the Black Sea coast dialects[[46]](#cite_note-matras_proceedings-46)
* [Vlax Romani dialects](/wiki/Vlax_Romani_language "Vlax Romani language"), chiefly associated with the historical Wallachian and Transylvanian regions, with outmigrants in various regions throughout Europe and beyond[[46]](#cite_note-matras_proceedings-46)

In a series of articles (beginning in 1982) linguist [Marcel Courthiade](/wiki/Marcel_Courthiade "Marcel Courthiade") proposed a different kind of classification. He concentrates on the dialectal diversity of Romani in three successive strata of expansion, using the criteria of phonological and grammatical changes. Finding the common linguistic features of the dialects, he presents the historical evolution from the first stratum (the dialects closest to the Anatolian Romani of the 13th century) to the second and third strata. He also names as "pogadialects" (after the **[Pogadi](/wiki/Pogadi "Pogadi")** dialect of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain")) those with only a Romani vocabulary grafted into a non-Romani language (normally referred to as [Para-Romani](/wiki/Para-Romani "Para-Romani")).

A table of some dialectal differences:

| First stratum | Second stratum | Third stratum |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *phirdom, phirdyom* *phirdyum, phirjum* | *phirdem* | *phirdem* |
| *guglipe(n)/guglipa* *guglibe(n)/gugliba* | *guglipe(n)/guglipa* *guglibe(n)/gugliba* | *guglimos* |
| *pani* *khoni*  *kuni* | *pai, payi* *khoi, khoyi*  *kui, kuyi* | *pai, payi* *khoi, khoyi*  *kui, kuyi* |
| *ćhib* | *shib* | *shib* |
| *jeno* | *zheno* | *zheno* |
| *po* | *po/mai* | *mai* |

The first stratum includes the oldest dialects: **Mećkari** (of [Tirana](/wiki/Tirana "Tirana")), **Kabuʒi** (of [Korça](/wiki/Kor%C3%A7a "Korça")), **Xanduri**, **Drindari**, **Erli**, **Arli**, **Bugurji**, **Mahaʒeri** (of [Pristina](/wiki/Pristina "Pristina")), **Ursari** (**Rićhinari**), **Spoitori** (**[Xoraxane](/wiki/Xoraxane "Xoraxane")**), **Karpatichi**, **Polska Roma**, **Kaale** (from [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland")), **Sinto-manush**, and the so-called *[Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_countries "Baltic countries") dialects*.

In the second there are **Ćergari** (of [Podgorica](/wiki/Podgorica "Podgorica")), **Gurbeti**, **Jambashi**, **Fichiri**, **Filipiʒi** (of [Agia Varvara](/wiki/Agia_Varvara "Agia Varvara"))

The third comprises the rest of the Romani dialects, including **Kalderash**, **Lovari**, **Machvano**.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Mixed languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Mixed languages")]

Main article: [Para-Romani](/wiki/Para-Romani "Para-Romani")

Some Roma have developed [mixed languages](/wiki/Mixed_language "Mixed language") (chiefly by retaining Romani [lexical items](/wiki/Lexical_item "Lexical item") and adopting second language grammatical structures), including:

* in [Northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe")
  + [Angloromani](/wiki/Angloromani_language "Angloromani language") (in England)
  + [Scottish Cant](/wiki/Scottish_Cant "Scottish Cant") (in Lowland Scotland)
  + [Scandoromani](/wiki/Scandoromani_language "Scandoromani language") (in Norway & Sweden)
* in the [Iberian Peninsula](/wiki/Iberian_Peninsula "Iberian Peninsula"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") and [France](/wiki/France "France"):
  + [Erromintxela](/wiki/Erromintxela_language "Erromintxela language") (in the [Basque Country](/wiki/Basque_Country_(greater_region) "Basque Country (greater region)"))
  + [Caló](/wiki/Cal%C3%B3_language "Caló language") (in [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") and [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain")).[[48]](#cite_note-48)
  + [Manouche](/wiki/Sinti-Manouche "Sinti-Manouche") (a variant of Sinte Romani in France and its Mediterranean borders from Spain to Italy)
* in [Southeast Europe](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe")
  + [Romano-Greek](/wiki/Romano-Greek_language "Romano-Greek language")
  + [Romano-Serbian](/wiki/Romano-Serbian_language "Romano-Serbian language")

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

Romani is the only Indo-Aryan language spoken almost exclusively in Europe.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Definitions-49)

The most concentrated areas of Romani speakers are found in the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") and central Europe, particularly in Romania, Bulgaria, North Macedonia and Slovakia.[[50]](#cite_note-termcoord-50) Although there are no reliable figures for the exact number of Romani speakers, the estimated amount of Romani speakers in the European Union is around 3.5 million, this makes it the largest spoken minority language in the European Union.[[50]](#cite_note-termcoord-50)

## Status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Status")]

See also: [Romani language standardization](/wiki/Romani_language_standardization "Romani language standardization")

The language is recognized as a minority language in many countries. At present the only places in the world where Romani is employed as an official language are the [Republic of Kosovo](/wiki/Republic_of_Kosovo "Republic of Kosovo") (only regionally, not nationally)[[67]](#cite_note-68) and the [Šuto Orizari Municipality](/wiki/%C5%A0uto_Orizari_Municipality "Šuto Orizari Municipality") within the administrative borders of [Skopje](/wiki/Skopje "Skopje"), [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia "North Macedonia")'s capital.

Portions and selections of [the Bible have been translated to many different forms of the Romani language](/wiki/Bible_translations_into_Romani "Bible translations into Romani").[[68]](#cite_note-69) The entire Bible has been translated to [Kalderash Romani](/wiki/Kalderash_Romani_language "Kalderash Romani language").[[69]](#cite_note-70)

Attempts to publish in Romani were undertaken in the interwar [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") (using the [Cyrillic script](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script")) and in socialist [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia").[[70]](#cite_note-71)

Some traditional communities have expressed opposition to codifying Romani or having it used in public functions.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Definitions-49) However, the mainstream trend has been towards standardization.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Definitions-49)

Different variants of the language are now in the process of being codified in those countries with high Romani populations (for example, [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia")). There are also some attempts currently aimed at the creation of a [unified standard language](/wiki/Romani_language_standardization "Romani language standardization").

A standardized form of Romani is used in Serbia, and in Serbia's autonomous province of Vojvodina, Romani is one of the officially recognized languages of minorities having its own radio stations and news broadcasts.

In Romania, a country with a sizable [Romani minority](/wiki/Romani_people_in_Romania "Romani people in Romania") (3.3% of the total population), there is a unified teaching system of the Romani language for all dialects spoken in the country. This is primarily a result of the work of [Gheorghe Sarău](/wiki/Gheorghe_Sar%C4%83u "Gheorghe Sarău"), who made Romani textbooks for teaching Romani children in the Romani language.[[71]](#cite_note-72) He teaches a purified, mildly [prescriptive](/wiki/Prescriptive "Prescriptive") language, choosing the original Indo-Aryan words and grammatical elements from various dialects. The pronunciation is mostly like that of the dialects from the first stratum. When there are more variants in the dialects, the variant that most closely resembles the oldest forms is chosen, like **byav**, instead of **abyav**, **abyau**, **akana** instead of **akanak**, **shunav** instead of **ashunav** or **ashunau**, etc.

An effort is also made to derive new words from the vocabulary already in use, *i.e.*, **xuryavno** (airplane), **vortorin** (slide rule), **palpaledikhipnasko** (retrospectively), **pashnavni** (adjective). There is an ever-changing set of borrowings from [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language") as well, including such terms as **vremea** (weather, time), **primariya** (town hall), **frishka** (cream), **sfïnto** (saint, holy). [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi")-based [neologisms](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism") include **bijli** (bulb, electricity), **misal** (example), **chitro** (drawing, design), **lekhipen** (writing), while there are also [English](/wiki/English_language "English language")-based neologisms, like **printisarel** < "to print".

Romani is now used on the internet, in some local media, and in some countries as a medium of instruction.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Definitions-49)

In 2024, Romani was added to [Google Translate](/wiki/Google_Translate "Google Translate").[[72]](#cite_note-73)

## Orthography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Orthography")]

Main article: [Romani alphabets](/wiki/Romani_alphabets "Romani alphabets")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Brno%2C_K%C3%A1znice_Cejl%2C_b%C3%A1se%C5%88_v_rom%C5%A1tin%C4%9B.jpg/250px-Brno%2C_K%C3%A1znice_Cejl%2C_b%C3%A1se%C5%88_v_rom%C5%A1tin%C4%9B.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brno,_K%C3%A1znice_Cejl,_b%C3%A1se%C5%88_v_rom%C5%A1tin%C4%9B.jpg)

Romani poem by Jan Döme Horváth printed on wall at Cejl prison, [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno")

Historically, Romani was an exclusively unwritten language;[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Definitions-49) for example, Slovak Romani's orthography was codified only in 1971.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

The overwhelming majority of academic and non-academic literature produced currently in Romani is written using a Latin-based orthography.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

The proposals to form a unified Romani alphabet and one standard Romani language by either choosing one dialect as a standard, or by merging more dialects together, have not been successful – instead, the trend is towards a model where each dialect has its own writing system.[[75]](#cite_note-errc-76) Among native speakers, the most common pattern is for individual authors to use an orthography based on the writing system of the dominant contact language: thus [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language") in [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") in [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") and so on.

To demonstrate the differences, the phrase /romani tʃʰib/, which means "Romani language" in all the dialects, can be written as **románi csib**, **románi čib**, **romani tschib**, **románi tschiwi**, **romani tšiw**, **romeni tšiv**, **romanitschub**, **rromani čhib**, **romani chib**, **rhomani chib**, **romaji šjib**[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24) and so on.

A currently observable trend, however, appears to be the adoption of a loosely English- and Czech-oriented orthography, developed spontaneously by native speakers for use online and through email.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

## Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Phonology")]

The following is the core sound inventory of Romani. Gray phonemes are only found in some dialects.

Loans from contact languages often allow other non-native phonemes.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006The_sound_system-78)

### Consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Consonants")]

The Romani sound system is not highly unusual among European languages. Its most marked features are a three-way contrast between unvoiced, voiced, and aspirated stops, and the presence in some dialects of a second rhotic ⟨ř⟩.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006The_sound_system-78)

### Stress

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Stress")]

Conservative dialects of Romani have final stress, with the exception of some unstressed affixes (e.g. the vocative ending, the case endings added on to the accusative noun, and the remoteness tense marker).[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006The_sound_system-78) Central and Western European dialects often have shifted stress earlier in the word.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006The_sound_system-78)

## Morphology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Morphology")]

### Nominals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Nominals")]

Nominals in Romani are nouns, adjectives, pronouns and numerals.[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24) Some sources describe articles as nominals.

The indefinite article is often borrowed from the local contact language.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84)

#### Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Types")]

General Romani is an unusual language, in having two classes of nominals, based on the historic origin of the word, that have a completely different morphology. The two classes can be called *inherited* and *borrowed*,[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24) but this article uses names from Matras (2006),[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006-34) *ikeoclitic* and *xenoclitic*. The class to which a word belongs is obvious from its ending.

##### Ikeoclitic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Ikeoclitic")]

The first class is the old, Indian vocabulary (and to some extent [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), Armenian and Greek loanwords).[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24) The ikeoclitic class can also be divided into two sub-classes, based on the ending.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006-34)

###### Nominals ending in o/i

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Nominals ending in o/i")]

The ending of words in this sub-class is -o with masculines, -i with feminines, with the latter ending triggering palatalisation of preceding *d, t, n, l* to *ď, ť, ň, ľ*.[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24)

Examples:[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24)

* masculine
  + ***o čhavo*** – the son
  + ***o cikno*** – the little
  + ***o amaro*** – our (m.)
* feminine
  + ***e rakľi*** – non-romani girl
  + ***e cikňi*** – small (note the change n > ň)
  + ***e amari*** – ours (f.)

###### Nominals without ending

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Nominals without ending")]

All words in this sub-class have no endings, regardless of gender.

Examples:[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová1974-85)

* masculine
  + ***o phral*** – the brother
  + ***o šukar*** – the nice (m.)
  + ***o dad*** – the father
* feminine
  + ***e phen*** – the sister
  + ***e šukar*** – the nice (f.) – same as m.
  + ***e daj*** – the mother

##### Xenoclitic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Xenoclitic")]

The second class is loanwords from [European languages](/wiki/European_languages "European languages").[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24)[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová1974-85)[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras200273-86) (Matras adds that the morphology of the new loanwords might be borrowed from Greek.)

The ending of borrowed masculine is -os, -is, -as, -us, and the borrowed feminine ends in -a.

Examples from Slovak Romani:[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24)[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová1974-85)

* masculine
  + ***o šustros*** – shoemaker
  + ***o autobusis*** – bus
  + ***o učiteľis*** – teacher (m.)
* feminine
  + ***e rokľa/maijka*** – shirt
  + ***e oblaka/vokna*** – window
  + ***e učiteľka*** – teacher (f.)

#### Basics of morphology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Basics of morphology")]

Romani has two [grammatical genders](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender") (masculine / feminine) and two numbers (singular / plural).[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84)

All nominals can be singular or plural.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová19744V1,3-87)

#### Cases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Cases")]

Nouns are marked for any of eight cases; [nominative](/wiki/Nominative_case "Nominative case"), [vocative](/wiki/Vocative_case "Vocative case"), [accusative](/wiki/Accusative_case "Accusative case"), [genitive](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case"), [dative](/wiki/Dative_case "Dative case"), [locative](/wiki/Locative_case "Locative case"), [ablative](/wiki/Ablative_case "Ablative case"), and [instrumental](/wiki/Instrumental_case "Instrumental case"). The former three are formed by inflections on the noun itself, but the latter five are marked by adding postpositions to the accusative, used as an "indirect root".[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24)

The vocative and nominative are a bit "outside" of the case system[[86]](#cite_note-seb52-88) as they are produced only by adding a suffix to the root.

Example: the suffix for singular masculine vocative of ikeoclitic types is ***-eja***.[[87]](#cite_note-89)[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová197431V2,1-90)

* ***čhaveja!*** – you, boy (or son)!
* ***cikneja!*** – you, little one!
* ***phrala!*** – brother!

The oblique cases disregard gender or type: **-te** / **-de** (locative), **-ke** / **-ge** (dative), **-tar/-dar** (ablative), **-sa(r)** (instrumental and [comitative](/wiki/Comitative "Comitative")), and **-ker-** / **-ger-** (genitive).[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84)

Example: The endings for o/i ending nominals are as follows:

|  | sg. nom. | sg. acc. | sg. voc. | pl. nom. | pl. acc. | pl. voc. |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 'boy' (masculine) | **čhav-o** | **čhav-es** | **čhav-eja** | **čhav-e** | **čhav-en** | **čhav-ale** |
| 'woman' (feminine) | **řomn-i** | **řomn-ja** | **řomn-ije** | **řomn-ja** | **řomn-jen** | **řomn-ale** |

Example: the suffix for indirect root for masculine plural for all inherited words is **-en**,[[86]](#cite_note-seb52-88)[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová197443V4-91) the dative suffix is **-ke**.[[90]](#cite_note-92)[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová197460V7-93)

* ***o xuxur*** – mushroom
* ***xuxuren*** – the indirect root (also used as accusative)
* ***Nilaj phiras xuxurenge.*** – In the summer we go on mushrooms (meaning picking mushrooms)

There are many [declension classes](/wiki/Declension_class "Declension class") of nouns that decline differently, and show dialectal variation.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84)

Parts of speech such as adjectives and the article, when they function as attributes before a word, distinguish only between a nominative and an indirect/oblique case form.[[92]](#cite_note-94) In the Early Romani system that most varieties preserve, declinable adjectives had nominative endings similar to the nouns ending in *-o* (masculine *-o*, feminine *-i*) but the oblique endings *-e* in the masculine, *-a* in the feminine. The ending *-e* was the same regardless of gender. So-called athematic adjectives had the nominative forms *-o* in the masculine *and* the feminine and *-a* in the plural; the oblique has the same endings as the previous group, but the preceding stem changes by adding the element *-on-*.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras200295-95)

#### Agreement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Agreement")]

Romani shows the typically Indo-Aryan pattern of the genitive agreeing with its head noun.

Example:

* **čhav-es-ker-o phral** – 'the boy's brother'
* **čhav-es-ker-i phen** – 'the boy's sister'.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84)

Adjectives and the definite article show agreement with the noun they modify.

Example:

* **mir-o dad** – 'my father'
* **mir-i daj** – 'my mother'.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84)[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Syntax-96)

### Verbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Verbs")]

Romani derivations are highly synthetic and partly agglutinative. However, they are also sensitive to recent development – for example, in general, Romani in Slavic countries show an adoption of productive [aktionsart](/wiki/Lexical_aspect "Lexical aspect") morphology.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2002117-97)

The core of the verb is the lexical root, verb morphology is suffixed.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2002117-97)

The verb stem (including derivation markers) by itself has non-perfective aspect and is present or subjunctive.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84)

#### Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Types")]

Similarly to nominals, verbs in Romani belong to several classes, but unlike nominals, these are not based on historical origin. However, the loaned verbs can be recognized, again, by specific endings, which are Greek in origin.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2002117-97)

##### Irregular verbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Irregular verbs")]

Some words are irregular, like **te jel** – to be.[[96]](#cite_note-98)[[97]](#cite_note-99)

##### Class I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Class I")]

The next three classes are recognizable by suffix in 3rd person singular.

The first class, called I.,[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24)[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová197420V1-100) has a suffix **-el** in 3rd person singular.

Examples, in 3 ps. sg:[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová197420V1-100)

* **te kerel** – to do
* **te šunel** – to hear
* **te dikhel** – to see

##### Class II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Class II")]

Words in the second category, called II.,[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24)[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová197420V1-100) have a suffix **-l** in 3rd person singular.

Examples, in 3 ps. sg:[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová197420V1-100)

* **te džal** – to go
* **te ladžal** – to be ashamed, shy away.
* **te asal** – to laugh
* **te paťal** – to believe
* **te hal** – to eat

##### Class III

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Class III")]

All the words in the third class are semantically causative passive.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová197457V4,1-101)

Examples:[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová197454S-102)

* **te sikhľol** – to learn
* **te labol** – to burn
* **te marďol** – to be beaten
* **te pašľol** – to lie

##### Borrowed verbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Borrowed verbs")]

Borrowed verbs from other languages are marked with affixes taken from Greek tense/aspect suffixes, including **-iz-**, **-in-**, and **-is-**.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84)

#### Morphology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Morphology")]

The Romani verb has three persons and two numbers, singular and plural. There is no verbal distinction between masculine and feminine.[[101]](#cite_note-103)

Romani tenses are, not exclusively, present tense, future tense, two past tenses (perfect and imperfect), present or past conditional and present imperative.

Depending on the dialect, the suffix **-a** marks the present, future, or conditional.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84) There are many perfective suffixes, which are determined by root phonology, valency, and semantics: e.g. **ker-d-** 'did'.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84)

There are two sets of personal conjugation suffixes, one for non-perfective verbs, and another for perfective verbs.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84) The non-perfective personal suffixes, continued from [Middle Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Middle_Indo-Aryan "Middle Indo-Aryan"), are as follows:[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84)

Non-perfective personal suffixes

|  | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| sg. | **-av** | **-es** | **-el** |
| pl. | **-as** | **-en** | |

These are slightly different for consonant- and vowel-final roots (e.g. **xa-s** 'you eat', **kam-es** 'you want').[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84)

The perfective suffixes, deriving from late Middle Indo-Aryan [enclitic](/wiki/Enclitic "Enclitic") pronouns, are as follows:

Perfective personal suffixes

|  | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| sg. | **-om** | **-al** / **-an** | **-as** |
| pl. | **-am** | **-an** / **-en** | **-e** |

Verbs may also take a further remoteness suffix whose original form must have been **-as(i)** and which is preserved in different varieties as **-as**, **-ahi**, **-ys** or **-s**.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84) With non-perfective verbs this marks the imperfect, habitual, or conditional.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84) With the perfective, this marks the [pluperfect](/wiki/Pluperfect "Pluperfect") or counterfactual.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84)

##### Class I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Class I")]

All the persons and numbers of present tense of the word **te kerel** in East Slovak Romani.[[102]](#cite_note-szl38-104)

|  | sg | pl |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1.ps | **me kerav** | **amen keras** |
| 2.ps | **tu keres** | **tumen keren** |
| 3.ps | **jov kerel** | **jon keren** |

Various tenses of the same word, all in 2nd person singular.[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24)

* present – **tu keres**
* future – **tu kereha** (many other dialects use a future particle such as *ka* preceding the imperfective form: **tu ka keres*)*
* past imperfect = present conditional – **tu kerehas**
* past perfect – **tu kerďal** (**ker** + **d** + **'al**)
* past conditional – **tu kerďalas** (**ker** + **d** + **'al** + **as**)
* present imperative – **ker!**

##### Class II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Class II")]

All the persons and numbers of present tense of the word **te paťal** in East Slovak Romani.[[102]](#cite_note-szl38-104)

|  | sg | pl |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1.ps | **me paťav** | **amen paťas** |
| 2.ps | **tu paťaha** | **tumen paťan** |
| 3.ps | **jov paťal** | **jon paťan** |

Various tenses of the word **te chal**, all in 2nd person singular.[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24)

* present – **tu džas**
* future – **tu džaha**
* past imperfect = present conditional – **tu džahas**
* past perfect – **tu džaľom** (irregular – regular form of **tu paťas** is **tu paťaňom**)
* past conditional – **tu džaľahas**
* present imperative – **džaľa!**

##### Class III

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Class III")]

All the persons and numbers of present tense of the word **te pašľol** in East Slovak Romani.[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24) Note the added **-uv-**, which is typical for this group.

|  | sg | pl |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1.ps | **me pašľuvav** | **amen pašľuvas** |
| 2.ps | **tu pašľos** | **tumen pašľon** |
| 3.ps | **jov pašľol** | **jon pašľon** |

Various tenses of the same word, all in 2nd person singular again.[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24)

* present – **tu pašľos**
* future – **tu pašľa**
* past imperfect = present conditional – **tu pašľas**
* past perfect – **tu pašľiľal** (**pašľ** + **il** + **'al**)
* past conditional – **tu pašľiľalas** (**pašľ** + **il** + **'al** + **as**)
* present imperative – **pašľuv!**[[103]](#cite_note-105)

#### Valency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Valency")]

[Valency](/wiki/Valency_(linguistics) "Valency (linguistics)") markers are affixed to the verb root either to increase or decrease valency.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84) There is dialectal variation as to which markers are most used; common valency-increasing markers are **-av-**, **-ar-**, and **-ker**, and common valency-decreasing markers are **-jov-** and **-áv-**.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84) These may also be used to derive verbs from nouns and adjectives.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Morphology-84)

Romani makes use of [valency-changing](/wiki/Valency_(linguistics)#Changing_valency "Valency (linguistics)") morphology which increases or decreases the valency of its verbs.[[104]](#cite_note-106)

## Syntax

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Syntax")]

Romani syntax is quite different from most Indo-Aryan languages, and shows more similarity to the [Balkan languages](/wiki/Balkan_languages "Balkan languages").[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Syntax-96)

Šebková and Žlnayová, while describing Slovak Romani, argues that Romani is a [free word order](/wiki/Free_word_order "Free word order") language[[24]](#cite_note-mluvnice-24) and that it allows for [theme-rheme structure](/wiki/Theme-rheme_structure "Theme-rheme structure"), similarly to Czech, and that in some Romani dialects in East Slovakia, there is a tendency to put a verb at the end of a sentence.

However, Matras describes it further.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2002167–168-107) According to Matras, in most dialects of Romani, Romani is a [VO language](/wiki/VO_language "VO language"), with [SVO](/wiki/Subject-verb-object "Subject-verb-object") order in contrastive sentences and [VSO](/wiki/Verb-subject-object "Verb-subject-object") order in thetic sentences.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Syntax-96) The tendency of some dialects to put the verb in final position may be due to Slavic influence.

Examples, from Slovak Romani:[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHübschmannová19747par_1,1-108)

* **Odi kuči šilaľi.** – This cup is cold.
* **Oda šilaľi kuči.** – This is a cold cup.

Clauses are usually [finite](/wiki/Finite_clause "Finite clause").[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Syntax-96) Relative clauses, introduced by the [relativizer](/wiki/Relativizer "Relativizer") *kaj*, are postponed.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Syntax-96) Factual and non-factual complex clauses are distinguished.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatras2006Syntax-96)

## Romani in modern times

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romani_language&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Romani in modern times")]

Romani has lent several words to English such as *pal* (ultimately from Sanskrit **bhrātar** "brother"[[107]](#cite_note-OEED-109)). Other Romani words in general British slang are *gadgie* (man),[[108]](#cite_note-Beal-110) *shiv* or *chiv* (knife).[[109]](#cite_note-111) Urban British slang shows an increasing level of Romani influence,[[108]](#cite_note-Beal-110) with some words becoming accepted into the lexicon of standard English (for example, *[chav](/wiki/Chav "Chav")* from an assumed Anglo-Romani word,[[108]](#cite_note-Beal-110) meaning "small boy", in the majority of dialects).[[110]](#cite_note-112) There are efforts to teach and familiarise [Vlax-Romani](/wiki/Vlax_Romani_language "Vlax Romani language") to a new generation of Romani so that Romani spoken in different parts of the world are connected through a single dialect of Romani. The Indian Institute of Romani Studies, [Chandigarh](/wiki/Chandigarh "Chandigarh") published several Romani language lessons through its journal *Roma* during the 1970s.[[111]](#cite_note-lee-113)

Occasionally loanwords from other [Indo-Iranian languages](/wiki/Indo-Iranian_languages "Indo-Iranian languages"), such as [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi_language "Hindi language"), are mistakenly labelled as Romani due to surface similarities (due to a shared root), such as *cushy*, which is from [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") (itself a loan from [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") **xuš**) meaning "excellent, healthy, happy".[[107]](#cite_note-OEED-109)
